questions.question: Explain the difference between a process and a program with an example.
keyPoints: Program is a passive entity (file on disk) containing instructions
Process is an active entity - program in execution
Multiple processes can run the same program simultaneously
Example: Opening multiple Notepad windows - all use same program but are different processes

questions.question: List and briefly explain the five basic process states.
keyPoints: NEW: Process being created but not yet admitted to OS
READY: Process waiting in ready queue for CPU allocation
RUNNING: Process currently executing on CPU
BLOCKED/WAITING: Process waiting for I/O or event
TERMINATED: Process completed execution, resources not yet reclaimed

questions.question: What is Process Control Block (PCB)? Enumerate the information stored in it.
keyPoints: PCB is a kernel data structure representing a process
Contains: Process state, Program counter, CPU registers
Contains: Memory management info (page tables, limits)
Contains: Accounting info (CPU time used, limits)
Contains: I/O status information (open files, devices)

questions.question: Define context switching and explain why it is necessary.
keyPoints: Context switching is saving state of currently running process
Loads state of another process to be run on CPU
Necessary for multiprogramming and time-sharing
Allows multiple processes to share single CPU
Involves saving/restoring registers, PC, memory mapping

questions.question: Explain the difference between long-term and short-term schedulers.
keyPoints: Long-term scheduler: Controls degree of multiprogramming
Long-term selects processes from new state to ready queue
Short-term scheduler: Selects which ready process gets CPU next
Short-term operates frequently (milliseconds)
Long-term operates less frequently (seconds/minutes)

questions.question: What is CPU-I/O burst cycle? Explain its significance in process scheduling.
keyPoints: Process alternates between CPU bursts and I/O bursts
CPU burst: time process executes on CPU
I/O burst: time process waits for I/O operation
CPU-bound processes have long CPU bursts
I/O-bound processes have frequent short I/O bursts
Scheduling algorithms must balance different burst patterns

questions.question: Differentiate between zombie and orphan processes.
keyPoints: Zombie: Child process has terminated but parent hasn't read exit status
Zombie still occupies entry in process table
Orphan: Parent terminates before child completes
Orphan processes are adopted by init process
Both are special process states requiring OS handling

questions.question: Explain the concept of multi-tasking in operating systems. How does process management enable multi-tasking? Discuss the role of context switching and schedulers in achieving this.
keyPoints: Multi-tasking allows concurrent execution of multiple processes
Creates illusion of parallelism on limited CPUs
Process management handles: process creation, scheduling, termination
Context switching saves/restores process states rapidly
Schedulers decide which process runs: long-term, short-term
Dispatcher transfers CPU control to selected process
Requires efficient PCB management and queue handling
Trade-off between throughput and response time

questions.question: Draw and explain the process state diagram showing transitions between different states. Include all possible transitions and explain what causes each transition.
keyPoints: Diagram showing: NEW → READY, READY → RUNNING, RUNNING → READY
RUNNING → BLOCKED, BLOCKED → READY, RUNNING → TERMINATED
NEW → TERMINATED possible if admission fails
Transitions caused by: scheduler dispatch, I/O request, interrupt
I/O completion causes BLOCKED → READY
Time slice expiry causes RUNNING → READY
Need clear diagram with arrows and labels

questions.question: What is process scheduling? Discuss the three levels of schedulers in an operating system. Explain how they cooperate to manage process execution efficiently.
keyPoints: Scheduling: deciding which process gets CPU and when
Three levels: Long-term, Short-term, and Medium-term schedulers
Long-term: Controls degree of multiprogramming, admits processes
Medium-term: Swaps processes in/out of memory for optimization
Short-term: Selects from ready queue for immediate CPU execution
Dispatcher: Actually gives CPU to selected process
Need balance between responsiveness and efficiency
Schedulers cooperate using queues and state transitions

questions.question: Describe the components of a process. Explain how a process is represented in memory with a diagram showing text, data, heap, and stack sections.
keyPoints: Process components: Program code, Program counter, CPU registers
Memory components: Text section (code), Data section (globals), Heap (dynamic), Stack (temporary data/function calls)
PCB stores metadata about process
Memory layout: Text at low addresses, then data/heap growing up, stack growing down
Stack stores: function parameters, return addresses, local variables
Heap stores: dynamically allocated memory during runtime
Each process has separate address space for isolation
